policy_statement: Develop and promote model local laws and development regulations for clean energy siting via Clean Energy Communities and Climate Smart Communities, minimizing impacts on forests/agricultural lands and considering co-location.
mechanism_description: Developing and promoting model local laws and development regulations guides clean energy siting to enable renewable energy projects while minimizing impacts on forests and agricultural lands and supporting co-location with agriculture.
primary_causal_pathway: Siting/permitting rules → ↑ renewable project deployment → ↓ fossil electricity generation → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: Local governments standardize and streamline clean-energy siting and development regulations (e.g., zoning, setbacks, model ordinances, co-location guidance), lowering transaction costs and uncertainty for renewable developers.  
(2) Mediating physical/ecological process: More renewable capacity is built and connected to the grid, increasing zero/low-carbon electricity supply; land-impact provisions steer projects toward lower-impact sites and dual-use (agrivoltaics), reducing conversion pressure on forests/ag lands.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in lifecycle GHG emissions via displacement of fossil generation (primary); potential secondary benefit of avoiding land-use change emissions and protecting carbon stocks (context-dependent).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is increased renewable energy deployment that displaces fossil-fuel electricity generation, reducing GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: governance/siting regulation changes → more renewable deployment → fossil displacement → GHG reductions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although minimizing forest/agricultural impacts can protect carbon stocks (nature-based outcome) and improve land efficiency (resource efficiency), the mechanism’s core climate effect for any policy in this class is enabling renewables at scale, which is direct emissions mitigation.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a specific policy were purely about avoiding land conversion without materially enabling additional renewable deployment, it could shift toward Nature-Based Solutions; however, the canonical mechanism explicitly links siting regulations to enabling renewable projects, keeping mitigation dominant.  
4) Applies to ALL instances sharing the mechanism: Any “clean energy siting regulations to enable renewables (with land-impact minimization)” relies on increased renewable buildout as the main lever for climate impact.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., land-use carbon protection, agrivoltaics co-benefits, biodiversity) is deferred to policy-level evidence and quantified design details.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If land-protection constraints are so strict that they significantly limit renewable buildout and the main effect becomes conservation/avoided land-use change, categorization could shift toward Nature-Based Solutions; otherwise, mitigation via renewable deployment remains primary.
key_indicators: “Create model local laws and regulations”; “develop and promote model local laws and development regulations”; “through the Clean Energy Communities and Climate Smart Communities programs”; “minimize the impact of clean energy siting on forests and agricultural lands”; “consider colocational opportunities for agriculture and renewable energy generation”
co_benefits: Reduced ecological/land-use impacts from renewable siting (protecting forests/agricultural lands); support for agrivoltaics/dual-use agricultural productivity
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be interpreted as leaning toward “regulatory-mandate,” but the action is to develop/promote model laws (guidance/technical support) rather than require adoption, so classified as a planning-strategy with enabling directness.
